name: sqlx (Go)
what_it_does: Extensions to Go's standard database/sql that add struct scanning, named query parameters, and in-query expansion. sqlx sits between raw database/sql and full ORMs — you still write SQL, but struct scanning eliminates manual field mapping. Supports PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, and any database/sql driver. Popular choice for Go services that want SQL control without ORM magic.
use_cases: Scan SQL query results directly into Go structs using db:struct tags without manual field assignment
Use named parameters in queries (:name, :id) mapped from struct fields or maps for readable parameterized queries
Expand IN clauses dynamically with sqlx.In() to avoid manual slice-to-placeholder generation
Build Go database layers that write raw SQL but avoid the boilerplate of database/sql scanning
Use Get() and Select() for single-row and multi-row struct scanning as drop-in enhancements to database/sql
not_for: Automatic migrations — sqlx is query-execution only; use golang-migrate or goose for schema migrations
Query building — sqlx requires you to write SQL; use squirrel or GORM's query builder for dynamic query construction
Full ORM with relationships — GORM handles associations, hooks, and model lifecycle; sqlx is deliberately lower-level
best_when: You want to write raw SQL in Go with struct scanning convenience but without a full ORM's magic — the 'control with less boilerplate' sweet spot.
avoid_when: You need automatic migrations, complex query building, or ORM-style relationships — use GORM or Ent.

auth.notes: Library with no auth — database credentials are handled by the underlying driver DSN.

pricing.notes: Free and open source. Note: sqlx is lightly maintained — last major release was several years ago but it's stable and widely used.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: sqlx errors are database driver errors wrapped in Go errors — they include SQL state codes and driver-specific messages. Missing struct fields cause 'missing destination name' errors at runtime.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Read queries are idempotent. Write queries require application-level idempotency handling.
known_agent_gotchas: Struct tags use db:"column_name" not json:"" — agents generating structs must use the correct tag; mismatches cause silent zero-value scanning
NamedExec() with struct parameters scans ALL exported struct fields — extra fields that don't exist in query cause 'missing named parameter' errors
sqlx.In() modifies the query string and must be followed by sqlx.Rebind() for non-question-mark placeholder databases (PostgreSQL uses $1, $2)
Get() returns sql.ErrNoRows (not nil) when no row found — agents must check for this specific error to distinguish 'not found' from actual errors
sqlx is NOT safe for concurrent use on a single DB connection — always use connection pool (sqlx.DB) not single connections in production
The library has been in maintenance mode — some newer database features (pgx v5 improvements) require switching to database-specific drivers rather than sqlx

security_score_components.security_notes: Parameterized queries via NamedExec and standard Exec prevent SQL injection. Raw query interpolation still possible — agents must use parameterized forms. Library maintenance slowdown is a minor dependency hygiene concern.
